title: Minecraft
edition_pinned: Java Edition 1.21, Tricky Trials (2024) — pins the Crafter, trial chambers, Survival rules, and Java server surface in one release.
moat: Breaking and placing the same block set turns terrain into both resource stock and player-authored state.

loop_skeleton.description: Mine blocks, convert drops through recipes, place blocks to change the world, then use the changed world to reach another resource or project.

depth_mechanisms.name: block conversion loop
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: A block can be removed into inventory, transformed by a recipe, and placed back as shelter, tool support, route marker, or machine part.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If mined materials cannot return to the world as placed blocks, players cannot turn an expedition result into a build that changes the next expedition.

depth_mechanisms.name: recipe dependency graph
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Recipes turn gathered supplies into tools and stations; the crafting table opens the recipe set that supports later mining and building.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If every recipe is available without supplies or stations, route choice stops being constrained by what the player can craft next.

depth_mechanisms.name: night preparation clock
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Survival couples daytime gathering and construction to mob attacks at night, making shelter and equipment compete for the first resources.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If night adds no attack risk, first-day players have no survival deadline for choosing between shelter, tools, and exploration.

depth_mechanisms.name: redstone production circuits
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Redstone signals can trigger blocks such as the Crafter, so spatial layouts encode repeated item transformations.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If signals cannot trigger crafting or block actions, players cannot replace a repeated manual production step with a circuit they designed.

feel_signature: A mined block enters the inventory with a pop; the player chooses whether its next form is a tool, shelter wall, route marker, or circuit.
second_session_answer: The saved world contains unfinished builds, altered routes, stored materials, and machines whose next step depends on yesterday's placements.
packaging_lessons: Ship mining, one recipe chain, block placement, a day-night threat, and save-state before adding bosses or dimensions.
Give gathered supplies roles in recipes, tools, building blocks, and placement so each supply can serve more than one loop verb.
Expose automation as placeable world rules so production remains a spatial design problem.
anti_lessons: Do not copy the material count without the break-craft-place conversion loop; item volume cannot substitute for world transformation.
Do not make a required quest chain choose the next project; Minecraft's world and recipe constraints leave that choice with the player.

system_bom.note: break, collect, craft, and place must update one world state
recipes convert expedition drops into tools, blocks, and circuit parts
slots make carried blocks compete with food, tools, and expedition loot
health, hunger, mob attacks, and dropped inventory put built progress at risk
the returning player must receive the same saved world and its placed structures
tool and station requirements gate which blocks and recipes can be reached
a server lets players alter the same saved world and divide project work
inventory, recipe book, and crafting grids show inputs, missing supplies, and outputs

citations.claim: The moat and block-conversion depth entry rely on mining supplies, crafting from them, and placing blocks to build in Survival.
The recipe-dependency entry and crafting BOM row rely on recipes, supplies, the inventory grid, and the crafting table's recipe access.
The night-clock entry and win-loss-death BOM row rely on Survival resource collection, construction, and mob attacks at night.
The redstone entry and packaging lesson 3 rely on the 1.21 Crafter receiving redstone pulses and ejecting recipe results.
The edition pin includes trial chambers, trial keys, reward vaults, the Crafter, and new combat encounters in Java Edition 1.21.
The feel signature follows the documented sequence of gathering supplies, moving them through inventory and recipes, then placing crafted objects in the world.
Packaging lesson 1 follows Minecraft's documented starting sequence: gather wood, craft a table and tools, then build shelter for survival.
Packaging lesson 2 is supported by gathered supplies becoming building blocks, mining tools, armor, and crafting-station inputs.
The second-session answer and save-state BOM row rely on a Realm restoring the same world state when players return and retaining backups.
The multiplayer BOM row relies on Java servers and Realms supporting players who plan builds, explore, and fight bosses together.
The inventory and UI BOM rows rely on the recipe book showing recipes, supplies, missing inputs, and the crafting grid.
The progression BOM row relies on supplies and crafting stations determining which tools and recipes the player can produce.
Anti-lesson — material-count inference: the documented Survival loop joins collection, crafting, and placement, so material count without that conversion does not reproduce the mechanism.
Anti-lesson — required-quest inference: the official guide presents building, exploration, crafting, and fighting as player-selected paths rather than one required project sequence.
